name: Achraf Hakimi
audioHint: Achraf: the 'ch' is a 'sh' sound. Hakimi: stress on 'KEE', the 'H' is a deeper pharyngeal sound.
funFact: Hakimi (حكيمي) derives from 'Hakeem' meaning 'wise' in Arabic. Born in Madrid to Moroccan parents.

name: Aleksandar Mitrović
audioHint: Mitrović: stress on 'MEE'. The 'ć' at the end is a soft 'ch' sound, lighter than English 'ch'.
funFact: The suffix '-ović' means 'son of' in Serbian — Mitrović means 'son of Mitar/Dmitar'.

name: Alisson
audioHint: Alisson with double 's' — stress on 'LEE'. The final syllable is nasalized in Portuguese.
funFact: Full name: Alisson Ramses Becker. His middle name Ramses references the Egyptian pharaoh.

name: Alphonso Davies
audioHint: Alphonso: stress on 'FON'. A Spanish-origin name pronounced with English vowels in Canada.
funFact: Born in a refugee camp in Ghana to Liberian parents, Davies became a Canadian citizen at age 5.

name: Andrej Kramarić
audioHint: Andrej: Croatian form of Andrew, 'AHN-dray'. Kramarić: stress on 'KRAH', the 'ć' is a soft 'ch'.
funFact: 'Kramarić' derives from 'kramar' meaning 'shopkeeper/merchant' — a common Croatian occupational surname.

name: André Onana
audioHint: André: French pronunciation — 'ahn-DREH', not 'AN-dray'. Onana: stress on the second 'NAH'.
funFact: Onana is a common surname in Cameroon from the Ewondo ethnic group. It means 'he who comes from far'.

name: André-Frank Zambo Anguissa
audioHint: Commonly called just 'Anguissa' — ahn-GEES-sah. The 'u' is silent. Zambo is his middle family name.
funFact: Anguissa is from the Beti people of Cameroon. His full four-part name reflects Cameroonian naming traditions.

name: Antoine Griezmann
audioHint: Antoine = ahn-TWAN (French 'oi' = 'wa'). Griezmann = GREES-mahn — the 'ie' sounds like 'ee', final 'n' is nasal.
funFact: Antoine Griezmann has Basque heritage — he grew up near the Spanish border and speaks Spanish fluently, which influenced his move to Atlético Madrid aged 14.

name: Arda Güler
audioHint: Güler: the 'ü' is like French 'u' or German 'ü' — round your lips to say 'ee'. 'Güler' means 'laughs'.
funFact: 'Güler' means 'one who laughs/smiles' in Turkish. At 19, he's called the 'Turkish Messi'.

name: Aurélien Tchouaméni
audioHint: Aurélien = oh-ray-LYAHN. Tchouaméni = choo-ah-meh-NEE. The 'Tch' makes a 'ch' sound. Stress on the final syllable.
funFact: Aurélien Tchouaméni's name is of Cameroonian origin. He became one of the world's most expensive midfielders when Real Madrid signed him — a rapid rise from Bordeaux.

name: Bernardo Silva
audioHint: Bernardo: stress on 'NAR', the final 'o' is 'oo' in Portuguese. Silva: 'SEEL-vah'.
funFact: 'Silva' means 'forest' or 'woodland' in Latin — it's the most common Portuguese surname.

name: Bruno Fernandes
audioHint: In Portuguese, the final 's' in Fernandes is 'sh'. Bruno: 'BROO-noo', not 'BROO-noh'.
funFact: One of the most common Portuguese names. 'Fernandes' means 'son of Fernando' — a patronymic tradition.

name: Bukayo Saka
audioHint: Bukayo = boo-KY-oh (not boo-KAY-oh). Saka = SAH-kuh. Of Yoruba (Nigerian) origin.
funFact: Bukayo is a Yoruba name from Nigeria meaning 'adds to happiness.' Saka is also a Yoruba surname. His parents immigrated from Nigeria to north London — and his story is very much the story of modern English football.

name: Casemiro
audioHint: The 's' between vowels is voiced as 'z' in Brazilian Portuguese. Final 'o' is 'oo'.
funFact: Full name: Carlos Henrique Casimiro. 'Casemiro' is a nickname from his surname, spelled differently.

name: Christian Eriksen
audioHint: In Danish, Christian is 'KRIS-tee-an' not 'KRIS-chun'. Eriksen: stress on 'EH', the 'sen' is unstressed.
funFact: Eriksen means 'son of Erik'. He famously returned to football after a cardiac arrest during Euro 2020.

name: Christian Pulisic
audioHint: Pulisic: stress on 'LIS'. The family pronounces it 'puh-LIS-ick', not 'POOL-ih-sich'. Croatian origin.
funFact: The Pulisic surname is Croatian in origin. His grandfather Mate Pulišić emigrated from Croatia to the US.

name: Cody Gakpo
audioHint: Gakpo: starts with the guttural Dutch 'G' — like gargling. 'GHAK-poh' with a hard stop on 'k'.
funFact: His full name is Cody Mathès Gakpo. The Gakpo surname has Togolese and Ghanaian roots.

name: Daichi Kamada
audioHint: Daichi: 'dai' sounds like 'dye', 'chi' like 'chee'. Kamada: even syllable weight.
funFact: Daichi (大地) means 'great earth/land' in Japanese — a powerful given name.

name: Dani Olmo
audioHint: Olmo: 'OHL-moh', both syllables clear. Dani is a common Spanish short form of Daniel.
funFact: 'Olmo' means 'elm tree' in Spanish. Dani spent his formative years at Dinamo Zagreb in Croatia.

name: Darwin Núñez
audioHint: Darwin: Spanish pronunciation with rolled 'r'. Núñez: the 'ñ' is the 'ny' sound, like in 'canyon'.
funFact: Named after Charles Darwin. The 'ñ' in Núñez represents a distinct letter in Spanish, not just an 'n'.

name: Diogo Jota
audioHint: Jota: the 'J' in Portuguese is 'ZH' (like the 's' in 'pleasure'). Not 'HOH-tah' like Spanish.
funFact: 'Jota' is his surname — it's also the name of a traditional Spanish dance and the Portuguese word for the letter 'J'.

name: Dušan Tadić
audioHint: Dušan: the 'š' is 'sh'. Tadić: the 'ć' is a soft 'ch'. Both names have stress on the first syllable.
funFact: Dušan is a traditional Serbian name from the medieval Serbian emperor Stefan Dušan.

name: Dušan Vlahović
audioHint: Dušan = DOO-shahn (the 'š' = 'sh'). Vlahović = VLAH-hoh-vitch (the 'ć' = soft 'ch'). Stress on first syllable of each name.
funFact: Dušan in Serbian means 'soul/spirit.' Vlahović's name comes from Vlah, a historical term for Romance-speaking communities in the Balkans. He became Juventus' record signing in 2022.

name: Edson Álvarez
audioHint: Named after Brazilian legend Edson (Pelé's real name). Álvarez: stress on 'AHL'.
funFact: Named after Edson Arantes do Nascimento — Pelé. A popular naming tradition in Latin American football.

name: Eduardo Camavinga
audioHint: Camavinga: the stress is on 'VEEN'. Four syllables. The name has Congolese-Angolan origins.
funFact: Born in a refugee camp in Angola to Congolese parents. Became a French citizen and debuted at 16 for Rennes.

name: Emiliano Martínez
audioHint: Four syllables in Emiliano with stress on 'AH'. Known as 'Dibu' (DEE-boo) from the cartoon character.
funFact: Nicknamed 'Dibu' after the Argentine cartoon 'Dibu' because of his resemblance as a child.

name: Endrick
audioHint: The 'dr' blend is distinctly Brazilian — 'DREEK' not 'DRIK'. The nasal 'en' starts the name.
funFact: Full name: Endrick Felipe Moreira de Sousa. Only 17 when he signed with Real Madrid.

name: Enzo Fernández
audioHint: The 'z' in Enzo is a clear 'ts' sound. Fernández has the stress on the second-to-last syllable.
funFact: Named after Uruguayan legend Enzo Francescoli, same inspiration as Zinedine Zidane's first name.

name: Erling Haaland
audioHint: Haaland = HAW-lahn — the double 'aa' in Norwegian gives a broad 'aw' sound. Not HAY-land or HAH-land.
funFact: Erling Haaland was born in Leeds, England, while his father Alfie Haaland played for Manchester City — a quirky connection to his future club. The 'aa' in Norwegian names like Haaland, Aaberg, Aas always makes a long 'aw' sound.

name: Federico Chiesa
audioHint: Chiesa: the 'Ch' in Italian is a 'K' sound. The 'ie' is 'ee-eh'. It means 'church' in Italian.
funFact: 'Chiesa' literally means 'church' in Italian. His father Enrico Chiesa was also an Italian international.

name: Federico Valverde
audioHint: Valverde: both 'v' sounds are soft (between 'v' and 'b'). Stress on 'VEHR'.
funFact: Nicknamed 'El Pajarito' (The Little Bird) for his tireless running across the midfield.

name: Florian Wirtz
audioHint: Wirtz: the 'W' is pronounced as a 'V' in German. The 'i' is short. One syllable: VEERTS.
funFact: Became the youngest Bundesliga scorer at 17. His first name Florian derives from Latin 'florens' meaning 'flourishing'.

name: Franck Kessié
audioHint: Kessié: the accent on 'é' gives a clear 'AY' at the end. Three syllables: 'keh-see-AY'.
funFact: Known as 'Il Presidente' (The President) at AC Milan for his commanding midfield presence.

name: Frenkie de Jong
audioHint: De Jong: the 'J' is pronounced as 'Y' in Dutch. 'Jong' rhymes with 'song' but with a 'Y' start.
funFact: 'De Jong' means 'the young one' in Dutch — one of the most common Dutch surnames.

name: Gavi
audioHint: Simple two syllables. The 'v' in Spanish is softer, almost between 'v' and 'b'.
funFact: Gavi is a nickname — his full name is Pablo Martín Páez Gavira. 'Gavi' comes from his surname Gavira.

name: Gianluigi Donnarumma
audioHint: Gianluigi: four syllables, 'Gi' is 'J'. Donnarumma: double 'n' and double 'm' are both fully pronounced.
funFact: At 196cm, Donnarumma means 'woman of the don' — a Neapolitan surname. Debuted at AC Milan aged 16.

name: Giovanni Reyna
audioHint: Giovanni: Italian pronunciation with 'joh'. Reyna: Americanized to 'RAY-nuh', Spanish would be 'RAY-nah'.
funFact: Named after his father Claudio Reyna, also a US international. Giovanni means 'God is gracious' in Italian.

name: Granit Xhaka
audioHint: Xhaka = ZHAH-kah. The 'Xh' in Albanian makes a 'zh' sound (like the 's' in 'measure'). Granit = GRAH-nit. Albanian names, Swiss player.
funFact: Granit Xhaka is of Albanian-Kosovar heritage. 'Xh' in Albanian is always 'zh' — so Xhaka = ZHAH-kah. His brother Taulant also played professional football. He's Switzerland's captain and most capped active player.

name: Hakan Çalhanoğlu
audioHint: Çalhanoğlu: 'Ç' is 'ch', the 'ğ' (soft g) is silent and lengthens the preceding vowel. Four syllables.
funFact: 'Çalhanoğlu' roughly translates to 'son of the herald/announcer' in Turkish.

name: Hakim Ziyech
audioHint: Ziyech: the final 'ch' is a guttural 'kh' sound (like the Scottish 'loch'). Not 'zeech' or 'zee-ek'.
funFact: Ziyech is of Amazigh (Berber) origin. The name means 'beautiful' in the Riffian Berber language.

name: Henry Onyekuru
audioHint: Onyekuru = on-yeh-KOO-roo. Igbo name from southeast Nigeria. The 'ony' sounds like 'on-yeh', not 'OHN-ee'.
funFact: Onyekuru is an Igbo name from Nigeria. The Igbo language is tonal — the same word with different tones can have completely different meanings. Many Nigerian names encode blessings or family circumstances at birth.

name: Hirving Lozano
audioHint: Hirving: the 'H' is silent in Spanish. The name is unique — his father invented it. Lozano: stress on 'SAH'.
funFact: His father created the name 'Hirving' by combining letters he liked. No one else had this name before him.

name: Jackson Irvine
audioHint: Straightforward English pronunciation. Irvine: two syllables, 'ER-vin' not 'er-VINE'.
funFact: Born in Melbourne to a Scottish father. Captains the Socceroos with a distinctive headband.

name: Jamal Musiala
audioHint: Musiala: four syllables, moo-zee-AH-lah. The 's' is voiced like a 'z'. Stress on 'AH'.
funFact: Born in Stuttgart to a German-Nigerian family, raised partly in England. Chose Germany over England at youth level.

name: James Rodríguez
audioHint: James: in Spanish it's 'HAH-mes' (two syllables), not the English 'James'. The 'J' is an 'H' sound.
funFact: Named after James Bond by his football-loving father. The Spanish pronunciation makes it sound completely different.

name: Jonathan David
audioHint: David: French-Canadian pronunciation is 'dah-VEED', not the English 'DAY-vid'.
funFact: Born in Brooklyn to Haitian parents, raised in Ottawa. He speaks English, French, Haitian Creole, and some Portuguese.

name: Joshua Kimmich
audioHint: Joshua in German starts with a 'Y'. Kimmich: the 'ch' is the soft German 'ich' sound, like a hiss.
funFact: Despite being only 176cm, Kimmich is known as one of the most aggressive defenders in world football.

name: Joško Gvardiol
audioHint: Joško = YOSH-koh (the 'J' = 'Y' in Croatian, 'š' = 'sh'). Gvardiol = GVAR-dee-ol — stress on first syllable, the 'gv' cluster is unusual in English.
funFact: The 'gv' consonant cluster in Gvardiol doesn't exist in English. Croatian 'j' is always a 'y' sound. Joško became the world's most expensive defender when Manchester City signed him in 2023.

name: Jude Bellingham
audioHint: Bellingham = BEL-ing-um. Simple once you drop the 'h' (it's -ham like Buckingham, Tottenham). Three syllables.
funFact: The '-ham' ending in English place names (Birmingham, Bellingham, Tottenham) comes from Old English 'hām' meaning village or home — it's always pronounced 'um', never 'ham'. Jude turned from teenager to superstar at Real Madrid.

name: Jules Koundé
audioHint: Jules = zhool (French 'J' = 'zh' like the 's' in 'measure'). Koundé = koon-DAY.
funFact: Jules Koundé was born in Paris but represents France, not Benin (his father's heritage). He is known for his versatility — comfortable at centre-back or right-back.

name: Julián Álvarez
audioHint: The J is an 'h' sound in Spanish. Álvarez ends with a soft 'th' in Castilian Spanish, or an 's' in Latin American Spanish.
funFact: Nicknamed 'La Araña' (The Spider) for his ability to appear everywhere on the pitch.

name: Jérémy Doku
audioHint: Jérémy: French pronunciation with the 'zh' sound. Doku: two syllables, stress on 'KOO'.
funFact: Doku is of Ghanaian descent. The name 'Doku' means 'blessing' in several West African languages.

name: Kai Havertz
audioHint: Kai rhymes with 'eye'. Havertz: the 'v' is pronounced as 'f' in German, and the 'tz' is a single 'ts' sound.
funFact: Kai is a name with origins in multiple cultures — Hawaiian (sea), German (warrior), and Chinese (victory).

name: Kalidou Koulibaly
audioHint: Kalidou = kah-lee-DOO. Koulibaly = koo-lee-BAH-lee. Both names have even, flowing syllables — stress the 'DOO' and 'BAH'.
funFact: Kalidou Koulibaly is named after the Islamic tradition of strong, meaningful names — Kalidou means 'the eternal' in Pulaar (Fula language). He was considered one of the best defenders in Serie A history during his time at Napoli.

name: Kaoru Mitoma
audioHint: Kaoru: three syllables with a soft 'r'. Mitoma: even stress, 'mee-TOH-mah'.
funFact: Mitoma wrote his university thesis on dribbling technique — then became one of the Premier League's best dribblers.

name: Kenan Yıldız
audioHint: Yıldız: the 'ı' (dotless i) in Turkish is a back vowel like 'uh'. 'Yıldız' means 'star'.
funFact: 'Yıldız' means 'star' in Turkish — a prophetic surname for the young talent at Juventus.

name: Kevin De Bruyne
audioHint: De Bruyne: the 'uy' is a unique Flemish/Dutch diphthong, closest to 'oy'. The final 'e' is a schwa.
funFact: 'De Bruyne' means 'the brown one' in Flemish Dutch, likely referring to hair or complexion.

name: Keysher Fuller
audioHint: Keysher: a unique Costa Rican name. 'KAY-sher' with stress on the first syllable.
funFact: Fuller scored Costa Rica's dramatic last-minute winner against Japan in the 2022 World Cup.

name: Kim Min-jae
audioHint: Straightforward Korean pronunciation. Min-jae: 'jay' at the end, not 'jye' or 'jeh'.
funFact: Nicknamed 'The Monster' for his imposing defensive presence. Kim (김) is the most common Korean surname.

name: Kylian Mbappé
audioHint: The 'M' in Mbappé is barely voiced — almost silent. 'm-bah-PAY'. The accent on the 'é' means it's a clear 'ay' sound, not dropped.
funFact: Kylian Mbappé's first name is of Celtic/Breton origin, from the name Killian. The 'M' in Mbappé comes from Cameroonian naming tradition — his father is Cameroonian.

name: Lamine Yamal
audioHint: Lamine: French/Arabic origin, three syllables. Yamal: stress on 'MAHL', the 'Y' is clear.
funFact: Born in 2007, Yamal is of Moroccan and Equatorial Guinean descent. Became Spain's youngest ever player at 15.

name: Lautaro Martínez
audioHint: Lautaro: the 'au' diphthong blends into 'ow'. Stress falls on 'TAH'. Martínez stress on 'TEE'.
funFact: 'Lautaro' is a Mapuche name from Chilean and Argentine indigenous culture, meaning 'swift hawk'.

name: Leandro Trossard
audioHint: Trossard: Flemish/Belgian pronunciation. The 'd' at the end is silent — 'TROSS-ar'.
funFact: Born in Genk, Belgium. Trossard is a Walloon surname, reflecting Belgium's French-Dutch linguistic divide.

name: Lee Kang-in
audioHint: Lee in Korean is closer to 'EE' than the English 'Lee'. Kang-in: the 'ng' connects smoothly to 'in'.
funFact: Lee (이) is the second most common Korean surname. He joined Valencia's academy at age 10.

name: Leroy Sané
audioHint: Sané: the accent on the 'é' gives a clear 'ay' sound. The 'S' is voiced like 'Z' in German.
funFact: His father Souleymane Sané was a professional footballer for Senegal. The surname has Senegalese roots.

name: Lionel Messi
audioHint: In Spanish, it's lee-oh-NEL, not LY-oh-nel. The surname is MEH-see, not MESS-ee.
funFact: The name 'Lionel' was inspired by American singer Lionel Richie. In Argentina, fans call him 'Leo' (LEH-oh).

name: Luis Díaz
audioHint: Luis = loo-EES (two syllables, stress on EES). Díaz = DEE-as in Colombian Spanish. The accent on 'í' confirms stress.
funFact: Luis Díaz comes from the Wayuu indigenous community in La Guajira, Colombia. His parents were both kidnapped in 2023 while he was playing in Liverpool's Champions League — one of football's most dramatic and heartbreaking stories.

name: Luka Modrić
audioHint: Modrić = MOH-dritch. The 'ć' in Croatian is a soft 'ch' sound (like 'ch' in 'cheap' but softer). Not 'MOH-drik'.
funFact: Luka Modrić grew up as a refugee during the Croatian War of Independence. He won the Ballon d'Or in 2018 — the first player in 11 years not named Messi or Ronaldo — after leading Croatia to the World Cup final.

name: Manuel Akanji
audioHint: Akanji: stress on 'KAHN'. The 'j' is pronounced as a soft 'j' (like 'jee'). Nigerian-Swiss heritage.
funFact: Born in Switzerland to a Nigerian father and Swiss mother. Akanji is a Yoruba surname meaning 'the one who conquers'.

name: Marcus Thuram
audioHint: Thuram: the 'Th' is just a 'T' in French, and the 'u' is the French 'ew' sound. Stress on 'RAHM'.
funFact: Son of Lilian Thuram, France's most capped player. The surname has roots in Guadeloupe.

name: Marquinhos
audioHint: Marquinhos = mar-KEEN-yos. The '-nhos' ending in Brazilian Portuguese makes a 'ny-os' sound (nasal). This is a mononym (one name).
funFact: Marquinhos is a nickname — his real name is Marcos Aoás Corrêa. In Brazilian Portuguese, '-inho/-inha' is an affectionate diminutive suffix, and '-inhos' is plural. Marquinhos = 'Little Marcos.'

name: Mateo Kovačić
audioHint: Kovačić: the 'č' is 'ch' (like 'church') and the final 'ć' is a softer 'ch'. Two distinct sounds.
funFact: Kovačić means 'little blacksmith' in Croatian — from 'kovač' (blacksmith) plus the diminutive '-ić'.

name: Matthijs de Ligt
audioHint: Matthijs: Dutch name where 'ij' sounds like 'ice'. De Ligt: the 'g' is the guttural Dutch sound, 't' is soft.
funFact: 'De Ligt' means 'the light' in Dutch. Matthijs is the Dutch form of Matthew.

name: Mehdi Taremi
audioHint: Mehdi: stress on 'DEE'. Taremi: stress on 'REH'. All vowels are clean and distinct.
funFact: Taremi is from Bushehr, Iran. The name Mehdi (مهدی) means 'guided one' in Arabic, an important name in Islam.

name: Memphis Depay
audioHint: Depay: stress on the second syllable, rhymes with 'pie'. He prefers to go by just 'Memphis' on his shirt.
funFact: Memphis dropped his surname from his shirt because he does not want to be associated with his father's family name.

name: Mike Maignan
audioHint: Maignan: the 'gn' in French is the 'ny' sound (like Italian 'gn' in gnocchi). Two syllables: 'men-YAHN'.
funFact: Maignan means 'tinsmith' in old French. He succeeded Gianluigi Donnarumma at AC Milan.

name: Mohamed Salah
audioHint: Mohamed = moh-HAM-med (not muh-HAM-id). Salah = SAH-lah (two equal syllables, not SAH-luh). The Arabic 'ح' (H) in Salah is emphatic.
funFact: Mohamed in Arabic is the most common name in the world. Salah (صلاح) means 'righteousness' or 'goodness' in Arabic. He's Liverpool's Egyptian King and became Africa's first mega-star of the Premier League era.

name: Mohammed Kudus
audioHint: Kudus: stress on 'DOOS' with a long 'oo'. Not 'KOO-dus' or 'KUD-us'.
funFact: Born in Nima, a diverse neighborhood in Accra. Kudus means 'holy' in Arabic.

name: Moisés Caicedo
audioHint: Moisés: the Spanish form of Moses, 'moy-SES'. Caicedo: 'kye-SEH-doh', the 'ai' is like 'eye'.
funFact: Caicedo is an Ecuadorian surname of Afro-Colombian origin, common along the Pacific coast.

name: N'Golo Kanté
audioHint: N'Golo: the apostrophe marks a syllabic 'N'. Kanté: nasal 'kahn' and the accent gives a clear 'TAY'.
funFact: His parents were Malian immigrants. The N' prefix in West African names indicates 'child of' or is a nasal consonant.

name: Neymar Jr
audioHint: Neymar: 'nay-MAR' with stress on 'MAR'. The 'R' at the end is the guttural Brazilian sound.
funFact: Full name: Neymar da Silva Santos Júnior. Named after his father, also called Neymar.

name: Nicolò Barella
audioHint: Nicolò: stress on the final syllable 'LOH'. Barella: double 'l' is pronounced distinctly in Italian.
funFact: Barella is Sardinian — born in Cagliari. His surname may derive from 'barella' meaning 'stretcher' in Italian.

name: Ousmane Dembélé
audioHint: Ousmane = oos-MAHN (West African name, stress on second syllable). Dembélé = dahm-beh-LAY — three syllables, stress on final 'LAY'.
funFact: Ousmane is a West African form of the Arabic name Uthman. Dembélé is a common Mande surname found across Senegal, Mali, Guinea, and the Gambia. He's known for his blistering pace and unpredictability.

name: Pedri
audioHint: Pedri = PEH-dree. In Spanish, the 'd' between vowels is softened. His full name is Pedro González López.
funFact: Pedri's real name is Pedro González López — Pedri is a Canarian nickname for Pedro, from his home island of Gran Canaria. He burst onto the scene aged 18 at Barcelona and was immediately compared to Xavi.

name: Rafael Leão
audioHint: Leão: the 'ão' is a nasal diphthong unique to Portuguese — sounds like 'own' spoken through the nose.
funFact: 'Leão' means 'lion' in Portuguese. Rafael is pronounced with a guttural 'R' in European Portuguese.

name: Raphaël Guerreiro
audioHint: Guerreiro = geh-RAY-roo. In Portuguese the 'ei' diphthong sounds like 'ay' and the final 'ro' is almost swallowed — geh-RAY-roo.
funFact: Guerreiro is a common Portuguese surname meaning 'warrior' (guerreiro). Born in France to Portuguese parents, he chose Portugal internationally. The French 'Raphaël' (rah-fah-EL) is slightly different from the English 'Rafael' (RAF-ee-el).

name: Rasmus Højlund
audioHint: Højlund: the 'øj' diphthong sounds like 'oy' in 'boy'. The 'd' at the end is silent in Danish.
funFact: 'Højlund' means 'high grove' in Danish — from 'høj' (high) and 'lund' (grove/woodland).

name: Richarlison
audioHint: Richarlison = hee-shar-lee-ZOHN. In Brazilian Portuguese, 'r' at the start = 'h'. The final '-son' is nasalized to 'ZOHN'. This is a single mononym (one name).
funFact: Like many Brazilian footballers, Richarlison goes by a single name. His full name is Richarlison de Andrade. The initial 'R' in Portuguese is an 'h' sound — Brazilians say 'hee-shar-lee-ZOHN.'

name: Robert Lewandowski
audioHint: Lewandowski = leh-van-DOF-skee. The 'w' in Polish is pronounced 'v'. Stress on 'DOF'.
funFact: In Polish, 'w' is always pronounced 'v' — so Lewandowski sounds like Leh-van-dov-ski. He scored 41 Bundesliga goals in a single season, breaking a 40-year-old Gerd Müller record.

name: Rodri
audioHint: The 'd' in Spanish 'Rodri' is soft (like 'th' in 'the'). Two syllables with stress on 'ROH'.
funFact: Short for Rodrigo Hernández Cascante. Won the 2024 Ballon d'Or — the first defensive midfielder since Matthias Sammer.

name: Rodrygo
audioHint: The 'R' at the start is the guttural Brazilian 'h' sound. The 'y' replaces the 'i' in the traditional spelling Rodrigo.
funFact: His full name is Rodrygo Silva de Goes. The unusual 'y' spelling was chosen by his parents to be unique.

name: Ronald Araújo
audioHint: Araújo: the 'ú' is stressed, and the 'j' is an 'h' sound in Spanish/Portuguese. Three syllables: ah-RAH-oo-hoh.
funFact: Araújo is a Portuguese/Galician surname meaning 'from Araújo', a place name. Ronald is the English form of Ronaldo.

name: Ryan Gravenberch
audioHint: Gravenberch: guttural Dutch 'G', and the final 'ch' is the hard Dutch 'kh' sound.
funFact: The Gravenberch name has Surinamese-Dutch origins. Ryan was born in Amsterdam's Ajax academy.

name: Sadio Mané
audioHint: Mané = mah-NAY — the accent on the 'é' means a clear 'ay' ending, not dropped. Sadio = SAH-dee-oh.
funFact: Sadio Mané's name is common across Senegal and Guinea. He grew up in Bambali, a village of 2,000 people. He has built a hospital and school in his hometown with his football earnings.

name: Salem Al-Dawsari
audioHint: Al-Dawsari: 'Al' is the Arabic article 'the'. Dawsari refers to the Dawasir tribe of Saudi Arabia.
funFact: Al-Dawsari scored the winning goal against Argentina in the 2022 World Cup — one of the greatest World Cup upsets.

name: Sandro Tonali
audioHint: Tonali: stress on the second syllable 'NAH'. Clean Italian pronunciation throughout.
funFact: Often compared to Andrea Pirlo due to his playing style and similar long-haired appearance.

name: Sardar Azmoun
audioHint: Sardar: a Persian title meaning 'commander'. Azmoun: stress on 'MOON' with a long 'oo' sound.
funFact: Nicknamed 'The Iranian Messi'. Sardar (سردار) means 'commander' or 'chief' in Persian.

name: Sebastian Szymański
audioHint: Szymański = shih-MAN-skee. The 'sz' in Polish = 'sh', and 'ń' at the end = slightly nasal 'n'. Think 'shih-MAN-ski.'
funFact: Szymański is Poland's most common surname suffix — the '-ski' ending is a hallmark of Polish names. Sebastian plays in Turkey for Fenerbahçe (feh-ner-BAH-cheh).

name: Sofyan Amrabat
audioHint: Sofyan: the 'y' creates a palatalized sound. Amrabat: stress on 'RAH', all hard consonants.
funFact: Born in the Netherlands to Moroccan parents. His brother Nordin Amrabat also plays for Morocco.

name: Son Heung-min
audioHint: In Korean, the family name 'Son' comes first. Heung-min: 'HUNG' with a soft transition to 'min'.
funFact: Son (손) means 'grandchild' in Korean. His father Son Woong-jung was also a professional footballer.

name: Sébastien Haller
audioHint: Haller: French pronunciation — the 'H' is silent and the 'er' is 'AIR'. Not 'HAL-er'.
funFact: Born in France to an Ivorian mother. Recovered from testicular cancer in 2022 and returned to professional football.

name: Takefusa Kubo
audioHint: Japanese names have even stress across syllables. Kubo: 'KOO-boh' with equal weight on each syllable.
funFact: Kubo joined Barcelona's La Masia academy at age 10, earning the nickname 'the Japanese Messi'.

name: Thibaut Courtois
audioHint: Thibaut: French pronunciation, the 'th' is just a 't', and the final 't' is silent. Courtois: 'TWAH' at the end.
funFact: 'Courtois' means 'courteous' in French. Thibaut is an old French form of Theobald, meaning 'bold people'.

name: Toni Kroos
audioHint: Kroos: one syllable, rhymes with 'gross'. The 'oo' is a long 'oh' sound, not like 'cruise'.
funFact: Kroos means 'curly' in Low German dialect — an ironic surname for the famously composed midfielder.

name: Victor Osimhen
audioHint: Osimhen = oh-SEE-m-hen. The 'mh' is not silent — say both letters lightly: 'see-m-hen'. Of Edo/Nigerian origin.
funFact: Victor Osimhen's name is from the Edo language of southern Nigeria. The 'mh' cluster is common in Edo names. He became Serie A's top scorer and one of the most expensive African players ever.

name: Vinícius Júnior
audioHint: Vinícius = vee-NEE-see-us (4 syllables, stress on 'NEE'). Júnior = ZHOO-nyor in Portuguese. The 'J' in Portuguese = 'zh'.
funFact: Vinícius Júnior's first name comes from Latin 'Vinicius.' In Brazilian Portuguese, the 'J' is always a 'zh' sound — Junior = ZHOO-nyor. He's celebrated for his samba-style dribbling, which he says comes from the baile funk culture.

name: Virgil van Dijk
audioHint: Van Dijk: the 'ij' is a Dutch diphthong pronounced like 'eye'. The 'D' is soft. The 'g' in Virgil is a guttural Dutch 'g'.
funFact: 'Dijk' means 'dike/levee' in Dutch — fitting for a defensive wall. The 'ij' digraph is sometimes written as 'ÿ'.

name: Wataru Endō
audioHint: Wataru: the 'r' is a soft tap. Endō: the macron on 'ō' means a long 'oh' sound.
funFact: The surname Endō (遠藤) means 'distant wisteria' — wisteria-based surnames are very common in Japan.

name: Weston McKennie
audioHint: McKennie: stress on 'KEN', ends with 'ee'. Not 'muh-KEN-eye' or 'MAC-ken-ee'.
funFact: McKennie grew up in a military family, living in Germany as a child — which helped his Bundesliga transition.

name: Wojciech Szczęsny
audioHint: Wojciech = VOY-chekh (the 'j' = 'y', 'ci' = 'ch', 'ch' = Scottish 'loch' sound). Szczęsny = SHCHEN-shnih (the 'szcz' cluster = 'shch'). One of the hardest surnames in football.
funFact: Szczęsny means 'happy/fortunate' in Polish — 'szczęście' (SHCHEN-shcheh) means happiness. He retired from the Polish national team but came back for the 2026 qualifying cycle.

name: Xavi Simons
audioHint: Xavi: pronounced like the Spanish 'Xavi' (Shah-vee), not 'Zah-vee'. Named after Xavi Hernández.
funFact: Born in Amsterdam to a Dutch-Spanish family. Named after FC Barcelona legend Xavi Hernández.

name: Xherdan Shaqiri
audioHint: Xherdan = ZHER-dan (Albanian 'Xh' = 'zh', like the 's' in 'vision'). Shaqiri = shuh-KEER-ee.
funFact: Xherdan Shaqiri is of Kosovar-Albanian heritage, born in Kosovo, raised in Switzerland. He scored one of the most celebrated goals of the 2018 World Cup with a bicycle kick vs Serbia — and celebrated with an Albanian eagle gesture.

name: Youssef En-Nesyri
audioHint: Youssef: 'YOO-sef' not 'yoo-SEF'. En-Nesyri: the 'En-' prefix is a Berber family name marker.
funFact: En-Nesyri scored Morocco's historic goal against Portugal in the 2022 World Cup quarterfinal.

name: Yunus Musah
audioHint: Yunus: 'YOO-nus' with the 'Y' pronounced clearly. Musah: 'MOO-sah' with a long 'oo'.
funFact: Born in New York to Ghanaian parents, raised in Italy and England. Chose the USMNT over England, Italy, and Ghana.

name: Ángel Di María
audioHint: Ángel has the stress on the first syllable, with a soft 'h' for the 'g'. Di María stress on 'REE'.
funFact: Named 'Ángel' after his birth on Valentine's Day — his mother saw it as a sign from angels.

name: İlkay Gündoğan
audioHint: İlkay: the 'İ' is a dotted i in Turkish, pronounced 'eel'. Gündoğan: the 'ü' is like French 'u', the 'ğ' lengthens the preceding vowel silently.
funFact: His surname Gündoğan means 'sun-born' in Turkish — from 'gün' (day/sun) and 'doğan' (born).
